questions.prompt: Instrument that separates ions by mass-to-charge ratio to yield isotope abundance.
questions.explanation: The mass spectrometer was developed by J.J. Thomson (1912) and refined by F.W. Aston (1919, Nobel 1922). A sample is vaporized, ionized (usually electron impact), accelerated, and deflected by a magnetic field — heavier ions deflect less, so detection gives a spectrum of m/z peaks with relative abundance. AP Chemistry Unit 1 uses these spectra to (1) identify isotopes by peak position, (2) calculate weighted average atomic mass from abundances, and (3) confirm element identity. The 2014 AP exam free-response Q2 was entirely a mass-spec analysis of magnesium isotopes — a near-annual fixture.

questions.prompt: Quantity calculated as the weighted mean of isotope masses times fractional abundances.
questions.explanation: Average atomic mass = Σ(isotope mass × fractional abundance), expressed in atomic mass units (u, formerly amu). Example: chlorine is 75.77% Cl-35 (34.969 u) and 24.23% Cl-37 (36.966 u), giving 35.45 u. This is the value printed on the periodic table — it is not the mass of any single atom but a population-weighted statistical mean. AP Chemistry Unit 1 routinely asks students to compute average atomic mass from a mass spectrum or work backward to find abundance given the published atomic mass and two isotope masses.

questions.prompt: Atoms with the same atomic number but different neutron counts and mass numbers.
questions.explanation: Isotopes share atomic number Z (same proton count, same element identity, same chemistry) but differ in neutron number N, so they have different mass numbers A = Z + N. Frederick Soddy coined the term in 1913 and won the 1921 Nobel for radiochemistry. Carbon has three natural isotopes — C-12 (98.9%), C-13 (1.1%), and trace C-14 (used in radiometric dating). Because chemistry depends on electron configuration, isotopes react nearly identically (kinetic isotope effects aside). AP Chemistry Unit 1 expects fluency in reading m/z peaks as isotopes, not different elements.

questions.prompt: Peak intensity ratio that gives the percent natural abundance of an isotope.
questions.explanation: Relative abundance is the percentage of a given isotope in a natural sample of an element, read directly from peak heights (or peak areas) on a mass spectrum. The base peak (tallest) is set to 100% and others scaled accordingly. For Cu, the spectrum shows two peaks at m/z 63 (69.2%) and m/z 65 (30.8%), yielding average mass 63.55 u. AP Chemistry Unit 1 free-response routinely demands students extract abundances from a labeled spectrum, then verify by computing the periodic-table mass — a high-yield 2-3 point question every cycle.

questions.prompt: Number of subatomic particles in a 35-Cl atom: 17 protons, 17 electrons, this many neutrons.
questions.explanation: For any isotope, neutron count = mass number (A) − atomic number (Z). For Cl-35, that is 35 − 17 = 18 neutrons. Chlorine's other major isotope, Cl-37, has 37 − 17 = 20 neutrons. The protons (17) define the element; neutrons control mass and nuclear stability. AP Chemistry Unit 1 free-response often pairs this calculation with isotope notation (e.g., ³⁵₁₇Cl) and asks students to also identify the ion charge if it differs from a neutral atom — chloride Cl⁻ adds one electron without changing nucleon counts.

questions.prompt: Shorthand like 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ specifying which orbitals hold an atom's electrons.
questions.explanation: Electron configuration lists each occupied orbital with the number of electrons it contains, filling lowest-energy orbitals first per the aufbau principle. The 1925 Pauli exclusion principle limits each orbital to two electrons of opposite spin. Hund's rule (1927) says degenerate orbitals (e.g., the three 2p) fill singly before pairing. Iron is [Ar] 4s² 3d⁶. AP Chemistry Unit 1 routinely asks students to (1) write a ground-state configuration, (2) identify the element from a configuration, or (3) explain anomalies (Cr, Cu). Noble-gas core notation is acceptable and expected for elements past Ar.

questions.prompt: Rule directing single occupation of degenerate p or d orbitals before any pairing occurs.
questions.explanation: Friedrich Hund's 1927 rule of maximum multiplicity says that for degenerate orbitals (same energy), electrons occupy each singly with parallel spins before any pair up. Lower electron-electron repulsion and exchange-stabilization make the parallel-spin arrangement lower in energy. Nitrogen's 2p³ is therefore three unpaired electrons (↑ ↑ ↑), not (↑↓ ↑ ). AP Chemistry Unit 1 routinely asks students to draw orbital-box diagrams and identify the number of unpaired electrons — a frequent free-response setup linked to paramagnetism and Coulombic stability arguments.

questions.prompt: PES peak-height ratio for the 2p subshell of fluorine vs. its 1s peak.
questions.explanation: Fluorine has electron configuration 1s² 2s² 2p⁵, so its PES spectrum shows three peaks: 1s (2 electrons), 2s (2 electrons), 2p (5 electrons). The 2p:1s peak-height ratio is 5:2 = 2.5. The 2p peak appears at lower binding energy than the 1s because 2p electrons are farther from the nucleus and more shielded. AP Chemistry Unit 1 free-response routinely asks students to (1) identify an element from a PES spectrum, (2) compute peak-height ratios as evidence, and (3) explain why a specific peak appears at the binding energy shown — students must invoke Zeff and orbital location.

questions.prompt: Exception giving Cr the configuration [Ar] 4s¹ 3d⁵ instead of [Ar] 4s² 3d⁴.
questions.explanation: Cr and Cu show anomalous configurations because half-filled (d⁵) and fully filled (d¹⁰) subshells are extra stable due to maximized exchange energy and minimized repulsion. Cr is [Ar] 4s¹ 3d⁵ (not 4s² 3d⁴) and Cu is [Ar] 4s¹ 3d¹⁰ (not 4s² 3d⁹). One 4s electron promotes to 3d to achieve the symmetric arrangement. AP Chemistry Unit 1 expects students to know both anomalies and to use them when predicting ionization or magnetic properties; the 2019 free response Q4 hinged on recognizing Cr's 6 unpaired electrons.

questions.prompt: Element identified by PES peaks at 273, 16.6, 11.5, and 1.51 MJ/mol with ratios 2:2:6:1.
questions.explanation: Sodium (Z = 11) has configuration 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s¹, predicting four PES peaks with electron counts 2, 2, 6, 1 — exactly the 2:2:6:1 ratio described. The 1s appears at the highest binding energy (~273 MJ/mol for Na's tightly bound innermost electrons), 2s next (~16.6 MJ/mol), 2p (~11.5 MJ/mol), and the lone 3s valence electron lowest (~1.51 MJ/mol). AP Chemistry Unit 1 free-response Q4 in 2017 was almost exactly this prompt — students had to identify Na, write its configuration, and justify peak heights using subshell-occupancy logic, a 4-point question.

questions.prompt: Trend in atomic radius that decreases across a period and increases down a group.
questions.explanation: Atomic radius decreases across a period because effective nuclear charge (Zeff) rises with each added proton while electrons fill the same shell — pulling the cloud inward. It increases down a group because each row adds a new principal quantum shell (n), and inner electrons shield outer ones from the nucleus. So F is much smaller than Cs. AP Chemistry Unit 1 expects students to rank atoms by size using only the periodic table and explain each ranking with Coulombic attraction, Zeff, and shielding language — a high-yield short-answer prompt every year.

questions.prompt: Rule that no two electrons in an atom can share all four quantum numbers.
questions.explanation: Wolfgang Pauli proposed the exclusion principle in 1925 (Nobel 1945). It states that no two fermions — including electrons — in an atom can have an identical set of four quantum numbers (n, l, ml, ms). Practically, each orbital holds at most two electrons, and they must have opposite spins (ms = +½ and −½). Pauli underlies the periodic table's structure: each row begins when n increases and a new shell opens. AP Chemistry Unit 1 expects students to use Pauli to justify pairing arrows in orbital diagrams and to explain why s holds 2, p holds 6, d holds 10, f holds 14 electrons.

questions.prompt: Ability of an atom in a bond to attract shared electrons; F at 4.0 sets the top.
questions.explanation: Electronegativity (EN) measures an atom's pull on shared bonding electrons. Linus Pauling's 1932 scale runs from F (4.0) down to Cs/Fr (0.7). EN increases up and to the right on the periodic table — the same direction as IE and electron affinity, opposite to atomic radius. The difference in EN between bonded atoms predicts bond polarity: ΔEN > 1.7 is largely ionic, 0.4–1.7 is polar covalent, < 0.4 is nonpolar covalent. AP Chemistry Unit 1 (and Unit 2) routinely asks students to use EN trends to rank bond polarity and predict dipole direction.

questions.prompt: Net positive nuclear pull experienced by valence electrons after inner-shell shielding.
questions.explanation: Effective nuclear charge (Zeff) approximates the actual pull a valence electron feels: Zeff ≈ Z − S, where Z is the atomic number and S is the screening constant from inner electrons (Slater's rules give estimates). For Na, valence electron Zeff ≈ 11 − 10 = ~1; for Cl, Zeff ≈ 17 − 10 = ~7. The much higher Zeff is exactly why Cl is so much smaller and so much more electronegative than Na, both with the same n=3 shell. AP Chemistry Unit 1 routinely demands a Zeff comparison to justify trends in radius, IE, and electronegativity.

questions.prompt: Energy released when a gaseous atom gains an electron; most negative for halogens.
questions.explanation: Electron affinity (EA) is the energy change when an atom gains an electron in the gas phase: X(g) + e⁻ → X⁻(g). Most elements release energy on electron capture (negative EA); halogens have the most negative values (Cl ≈ −349 kJ/mol). Noble gases and group 2/12 have near-zero or slightly positive EA — they don't want extra electrons. EA generally increases (becomes more negative) across a period and decreases down a group, mirroring atomic-radius trends. AP Chemistry Unit 1 contrasts EA from IE and uses both to predict the energetics of ion formation in salts.

questions.prompt: Force law stating attraction is proportional to q₁q₂/r².
questions.explanation: Coulomb's law, formulated by Charles-Augustin de Coulomb in 1785, states F = k·q₁q₂/r², where k = 8.99 × 10⁹ N·m²/C². For atoms, this governs the attraction between the nucleus (q₁ ≈ +Zeff·e) and a valence electron (q₂ = −e). Larger Zeff and smaller r both increase attraction. AP Chemistry Unit 1 expects students to invoke Coulomb's law explicitly when explaining trends in radius, ionization energy, electron affinity, and lattice energy. Rubrics award credit only when both the charge-product and inverse-square distance terms appear in the answer.

questions.prompt: Quantity that increases lattice energy when ion charges go from 1 to 2.
questions.explanation: Coulomb's law makes lattice energy roughly proportional to (q₁ × q₂)/r. Doubling each ion's charge multiplies the numerator by 4. That is why MgO (Mg²⁺O²⁻, lattice energy ~−3795 kJ/mol) has nearly four times the lattice energy of NaF (Na⁺F⁻, ~−910 kJ/mol), despite similar inter-ionic distances. AP Chemistry Unit 1 rubrics typically require students to compare (1) charges and (2) ionic radii when ranking lattice energies — both factors must be cited, and the charge factor usually dominates when the cation/anion identities differ in charge.

questions.prompt: Variable in the denominator of Coulomb's law: r-something.
questions.explanation: Coulomb's law has r² in the denominator: F = kq₁q₂/r². Doubling the distance between two charges cuts the force to one fourth. But — critically — when ranking ionic compounds by lattice energy, AP Chemistry uses U ∝ q₁q₂/r (energy, not force, so r¹). The r² appears only when discussing force or field. AP Chemistry Unit 1 routinely traps students who confuse the two. For atomic-trend explanations (radius, IE, lattice energy), r¹ in the denominator is acceptable as long as the student explains it as Coulombic attraction.

questions.prompt: Energy released when gaseous ions form a one-mole crystalline solid.
questions.explanation: Lattice energy is the energy change when gaseous cations and anions assemble into a mole of crystalline ionic solid: Na⁺(g) + Cl⁻(g) → NaCl(s), ΔH = −787 kJ/mol. It is always exothermic for formation; the more negative, the more stable the lattice. Coulomb's law predicts magnitude ∝ |q₁q₂|/r, so MgO ≫ NaF. Lattice energy controls melting point, hardness, and solubility trends for ionic salts. AP Chemistry Unit 2 and Unit 6 both call on lattice-energy reasoning, and the Born-Haber cycle in free-response problems decomposes ΔH formation into lattice + atomization + IE + EA.

questions.prompt: Concept explaining why second IE for Na is enormous compared to first IE.
questions.explanation: Removing the first electron from Na (3s¹) costs only 496 kJ/mol because the 3s electron is far from the nucleus and well-shielded. The second electron must come from the 2p orbital of the noble-gas-like Ne core — much closer to the nucleus, much higher Zeff, no shielding from 3s. So IE₂(Na) = 4562 kJ/mol, nearly 10× IE₁. The huge jump is direct evidence of shell structure and Coulombic attraction. AP Chemistry Unit 1 frequently presents a table of successive IEs and asks students to identify the group based on where the giant jump occurs (after 1 IE = Group 1; after 2 IEs = Group 2, etc.).

questions.prompt: Technique using X-rays or UV to eject and energy-analyze electrons from each subshell.
questions.explanation: Photoelectron spectroscopy (PES) was pioneered by Kai Siegbahn in the 1960s (Nobel 1981). High-energy photons hit a sample; ejected electrons' kinetic energies reveal binding energies of each orbital. The resulting spectrum shows peaks at each subshell's binding energy, with peak height proportional to electron count in that subshell. Higher binding energy = more strongly held = closer to the nucleus. AP Chemistry Unit 1 added PES to the redesigned CED in 2014 and now features it on roughly half of all free-response cycles — students must read a spectrum and assign each peak to a subshell.

questions.prompt: Energy required to remove the most loosely bound electron from a gaseous atom.
questions.explanation: First ionization energy (IE₁) is the energy to remove one mole of outermost electrons from gaseous atoms: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It rises across a period (rising Zeff, smaller radius, stronger pull on the leaving electron) and falls down a group (larger n, more shielding, weaker pull). Helium has the highest IE₁ (2372 kJ/mol); francium the lowest. Anomalies: B drops below Be (new p subshell), O drops below N (paired-electron repulsion in p⁴). AP Chemistry Unit 1 routinely tests both the trend and these two anomalies.

questions.prompt: Energy required to remove an electron from a specific orbital, plotted on PES x-axis.
questions.explanation: Binding energy is the energy holding an electron to its nucleus in a specific orbital, measured directly by PES as the difference between photon energy and ejected-electron kinetic energy: BE = hν − KE. AP Chemistry PES plots binding energy on the x-axis (usually in MJ/mol, increasing leftward by convention). Higher binding energy corresponds to inner-shell electrons (1s ≫ 2s ≫ 2p) and to elements with higher Zeff. AP Chemistry Unit 1 free-response cycles consistently test interpretation of PES peaks for binding energy, peak ratios, and element identification.

questions.prompt: Reason the 2s peak in an atom's PES appears at higher binding energy than its 2p peak.
questions.explanation: 2s electrons have higher radial probability density very close to the nucleus than 2p electrons — they 'penetrate' the inner 1s shell more effectively. Despite both being in the n=2 shell, the 2s electron experiences a larger effective nuclear charge, so its binding energy is greater. This is why 2s sits lower in energy than 2p (aufbau order) and why the 2s PES peak appears at higher BE on the spectrum (recall the x-axis convention). AP Chemistry Unit 1 rubrics demand the words 'penetration' or 'closer to nucleus' alongside Zeff for full credit on this question.

questions.prompt: Order of orbital filling captured by this principle: lower-energy orbitals fill first.
questions.explanation: The aufbau principle (German: 'building up') was formalized by Bohr and Pauli in the 1920s. Electrons fill orbitals from lowest to highest energy: 1s → 2s → 2p → 3s → 3p → 4s → 3d → 4p → 5s → 4d → 5p → 6s → 4f → 5d → 6p → 7s. The 4s fills before 3d because, for neutral atoms, the 4s orbital sits slightly lower than 3d due to penetration effects. Critically, when transition-metal cations form, electrons are removed from 4s first (not 3d) — so Fe²⁺ is [Ar] 3d⁶, not [Ar] 4s² 3d⁴. AP Chemistry Unit 1 tests this reversal as a near-annual conceptual trap.

questions.prompt: Bond formed by electron transfer producing oppositely charged ions.
questions.explanation: An ionic bond forms when a metal transfers one or more electrons to a nonmetal, creating a cation and anion that attract via Coulomb's law. EN difference is usually > ~1.7. The resulting lattice (NaCl, MgO, CaF₂) is held by 3D electrostatic forces, producing high melting points, brittleness, and conductivity only when molten or dissolved. Lattice energy U ∝ q₁q₂/r captures bond strength — MgO (≈ −3795 kJ/mol) ≫ NaCl (≈ −787 kJ/mol). AP Chemistry Unit 2 routinely asks students to rank ionic-compound melting points by charge and size, citing Coulomb's law.

questions.prompt: Sea of delocalized valence electrons explains malleability and conductivity in this bond.
questions.explanation: Metallic bonding is described by the 'electron sea' or band model: metal cations occupy fixed lattice positions while their valence electrons delocalize across the entire crystal. This explains high electrical and thermal conductivity (free-moving carriers), malleability/ductility (layers slide without breaking directional bonds), and metallic luster (electrons absorb and re-emit visible light). Bond strength scales with the number of delocalized electrons (Cs < Mg < W). Alloys like steel or brass are interstitial or substitutional mixtures whose hardness exceeds the pure metal. AP Chemistry Unit 2 contrasts metallic with ionic and covalent bonding via property comparisons.

questions.prompt: Bond with unequal electron sharing creating partial charges, like H–Cl.
questions.explanation: A polar covalent bond forms between atoms with 0.4 < ΔEN < ~1.7. The more electronegative atom carries a partial negative charge (δ⁻) and the other a partial positive charge (δ⁺). For HCl, ΔEN = 3.0 − 2.1 = 0.9 → δ⁺H–Clδ⁻, with a bond dipole pointed toward Cl. Bond polarity, summed as vectors over the entire molecule, gives net molecular polarity — but only if geometry is asymmetric (CO₂ has polar bonds but is nonpolar). AP Chemistry Unit 2 routinely asks students to compute ΔEN, draw bond dipoles, and decide if the molecule is overall polar.

questions.prompt: Side-by-side p-orbital overlap producing this bond above and below the bond axis.
questions.explanation: A π (pi) bond forms when two unhybridized p orbitals on adjacent atoms overlap sideways, creating bonding electron density above and below the internuclear axis. π bonds are weaker than σ bonds because the overlap is less direct. A double bond contains 1 σ + 1 π; a triple bond contains 1 σ + 2 π. π bonds prevent rotation about the bond axis (cis/trans isomers exist for C=C), and they shorten the bond and raise its strength relative to a single σ. AP Chemistry Unit 2 free-response routinely asks for σ and π bond counts in molecules like CO₂ (2 σ + 2 π), benzene (12 σ + 3 π), or HCN (2 σ + 2 π).

questions.prompt: Bond enthalpy estimate of ΔH for CH₄ + 2 O₂ → CO₂ + 2 H₂O using avg values.
questions.explanation: Methane combustion: ΔH ≈ Σ(bonds broken) − Σ(bonds formed). Broken: 4 C-H (4×413) + 2 O=O (2×495) = 1652 + 990 = 2642 kJ. Formed: 2 C=O in CO₂ (2×799) + 4 O-H in 2 H₂O (4×463) = 1598 + 1852 = 3450 kJ. ΔH ≈ 2642 − 3450 = −808 kJ (≈ −800 kJ). Experimental value is −890 kJ/mol; the discrepancy stems from using average bond enthalpies rather than molecule-specific values. AP Chemistry Unit 2 free-response routinely demands this calculation step-by-step, with rubrics requiring explicit bond accounting.

questions.prompt: Diagrams using dots for valence electrons to depict bonding.
questions.explanation: Lewis structures, introduced by G.N. Lewis in his 1916 paper 'The Atom and the Molecule,' depict valence electrons as dots and bonding pairs as lines. Atoms generally seek the octet (8 valence electrons total), with H seeking 2. To draw one: count total valence electrons, place least electronegative atom in the center, form single bonds, distribute remaining electrons as lone pairs, then form double/triple bonds to satisfy octets. AP Chemistry Unit 2 free-response routinely scores 1 point for a correct Lewis structure — a foundational skill before any geometry, polarity, or hybridization question.

questions.prompt: Bond formed when two atoms share an electron pair.
questions.explanation: Gilbert N. Lewis introduced the covalent (shared-pair) bond model in 1916. Two atoms with similar electronegativities share one or more electron pairs to approach a noble-gas configuration. Bond strengths range ~150 kJ/mol (F-F) to ~945 kJ/mol (N≡N). Bond length, strength, and order are inversely related: shorter bonds are stronger (triple > double > single for the same pair). AP Chemistry Unit 2 expects students to identify bond order, length, and strength from a Lewis structure and to predict molecular properties (BP, MP, solubility) based on covalent vs. ionic character.

questions.prompt: Eight-electron stable configuration around an atom in a Lewis structure.
questions.explanation: The octet rule states that main-group atoms (especially C, N, O, F) tend to achieve eight valence electrons via sharing, gaining, or losing electrons. It arises because a full ns² np⁶ configuration matches the next noble gas. Exceptions: H, Li, Be aim for 2; B and Al often have only 6 (electron-deficient, e.g., BF₃); period 3+ elements (P, S, Cl, Xe) can hold expanded octets of 10 or 12 (PCl₅, SF₆, XeF₄). AP Chemistry Unit 2 rubrics demand students cite the octet (or the relevant exception) when justifying a Lewis structure's validity.

questions.prompt: Equivalent Lewis structures that differ only in electron placement, like ozone or nitrate.
questions.explanation: Resonance occurs when more than one valid Lewis structure can be drawn for a molecule by moving only π electrons. The actual molecule is a hybrid (average) of all contributors, with delocalized electrons and bond orders intermediate between single and multiple. NO₃⁻ has three equivalent resonance structures, giving each N-O bond order 1.33 and length ~124 pm (between single and double). Benzene's two Kekulé structures yield six equivalent 1.5-order C-C bonds. AP Chemistry Unit 2 routinely asks students to draw all resonance contributors, average them, and predict bond lengths.

questions.prompt: Difference between an atom's group valence count and its electrons in a Lewis structure.
questions.explanation: Formal charge = (group valence electrons) − (lone-pair electrons) − ½(bonding electrons). It's a bookkeeping tool to choose the best Lewis structure when multiple are possible: the preferred contributor has (1) all formal charges as close to zero as possible, and (2) any negative charge on the most electronegative atom. For SO₄²⁻, the structure with two S=O double bonds and two S-O⁻ single bonds (S formal charge 0) is favored over the all-singles version (S formal charge +2). AP Chemistry Unit 2 free-response often asks students to compute and compare formal charges.

questions.prompt: Theory predicting molecular shape from electron-pair repulsion around a central atom.
questions.explanation: VSEPR — Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion — theory was developed by Ronald Gillespie and Ronald Nyholm in 1957. Electron pairs (bonding and lone) around a central atom repel each other and arrange themselves at maximum angular separation. Lone pairs occupy more angular space than bonding pairs (LP-LP > LP-BP > BP-BP repulsion), distorting ideal angles. The number of electron domains around the central atom dictates the electron geometry (2 = linear, 3 = trigonal planar, 4 = tetrahedral, 5 = trigonal bipyramidal, 6 = octahedral). AP Chemistry Unit 2 free-response routinely demands the steric-number rationale.

questions.prompt: Shape of water: two bonding pairs, two lone pairs, ~104.5°.
questions.explanation: Water (H₂O) has four electron domains around oxygen — two O-H bonds plus two lone pairs — so the electron geometry is tetrahedral but the molecular geometry is bent. The H-O-H angle compresses from 109.5° to ~104.5° because lone pairs repel more strongly than bonding pairs. This bent shape, combined with the polar O-H bonds, gives water its substantial dipole moment (1.85 D), driving its many anomalous properties (high BP, high heat capacity, high surface tension, density max at 4°C). AP Chemistry Unit 2 ties this geometry directly to Unit 3 IMF discussions.

questions.prompt: Geometry of PCl₅: five bonding pairs, no lone pairs on central P.
questions.explanation: Trigonal bipyramidal geometry has five bonding domains in a 'double-pyramid' arrangement: three equatorial positions at 120° (in a plane) and two axial positions at 90° to that plane. PCl₅ is the textbook example. Axial bonds are slightly longer than equatorial because they have three 90° neighbors (vs. two 120° plus two 90° for equatorial). Lone pairs prefer equatorial sites (less repulsion), giving derived shapes: seesaw (1 LP, SF₄), T-shape (2 LP, ClF₃), and linear (3 LP, XeF₂). AP Chemistry Unit 2 expects students to recall both ideal and lone-pair-modified geometries.

questions.prompt: Lewis exception in which the central atom holds more than eight electrons.
questions.explanation: Expanded octet (hypervalent) molecules occur when the central atom is in period 3 or below (P, S, Cl, Xe, etc.) and holds 10 or 12 electrons by accessing nominally vacant d-orbitals (modern theory increasingly invokes 3-center-4-electron bonds rather than d-participation). Classic examples: PCl₅ (10 e⁻, trigonal bipyramidal), SF₆ (12 e⁻, octahedral), XeF₄ (12 e⁻, square planar). Period 2 elements (C, N, O, F) cannot expand. AP Chemistry Unit 2 expects students to recognize when a central atom can violate the octet and to assign the correct VSEPR geometry that results.

questions.prompt: Shape and angle of XeF₄: square planar, this many lone pairs on Xe.
questions.explanation: Xenon tetrafluoride (XeF₄) has six electron domains around xenon: four bonding pairs (Xe-F) plus two lone pairs. The lone pairs occupy trans-axial positions (180° apart, minimizing LP-LP repulsion), leaving the four F atoms in a square planar arrangement at 90° angles. The molecule is therefore square planar and nonpolar (bond dipoles cancel by symmetry). XeF₄ was a landmark synthesis by Neil Bartlett's group in 1962, proving noble gases form compounds. AP Chemistry Unit 2 uses XeF₄ as a key case study for octahedral electron geometry with 2 lone pairs.

questions.prompt: Mixing of atomic orbitals to form equivalent bonding orbitals of intermediate character.
questions.explanation: Hybridization, proposed by Linus Pauling in 1931, mixes atomic orbitals on the same atom to form equivalent hybrid orbitals oriented along bond directions. The number of hybrids equals the number of atomic orbitals mixed. sp (1 s + 1 p, 2 hybrids, linear, 180°), sp² (1 s + 2 p, 3 hybrids, trigonal planar, 120°), sp³ (1 s + 3 p, 4 hybrids, tetrahedral, 109.5°). Higher principal-quantum-number atoms can use d-orbitals (sp³d, sp³d² — though modern MO theory often prefers other descriptions). AP Chemistry Unit 2 expects students to assign hybridization from electron-domain count.

questions.prompt: Geometry and bond angle of a methane molecule: 109.5° and this shape.
questions.explanation: Tetrahedral geometry has four equivalent bond domains at 109.5° angles. CH₄ is the classic example — carbon sp³ hybridized, four C-H bonds. Other tetrahedral species: NH₄⁺, SO₄²⁻, ClO₄⁻, SiH₄, CCl₄. Replacing a bonding pair with a lone pair yields trigonal pyramidal (NH₃, ~107°) or bent (H₂O, ~104.5°), with lone-pair repulsion compressing remaining bond angles. AP Chemistry Unit 2 free-response standards include drawing the geometry, labeling angles, and comparing related molecules (CH₄, NH₃, H₂O) to show how lone pairs reduce H-X-H angles below the ideal 109.5°.

questions.prompt: Hybridization on carbon in ethyne (acetylene), C₂H₂.
questions.explanation: In ethyne (HC≡CH), each carbon has 2 electron domains — one σ bond to H and one σ bond to the other C — so each C is sp hybridized. The two sp hybrids point 180° apart along the linear molecular axis. The two remaining unhybridized 2p orbitals on each C form two π bonds with the other C, completing the triple bond (1 σ + 2 π). Bond length 120 pm, bond enthalpy ~835 kJ/mol. AP Chemistry Unit 2 routinely uses ethyne, ethene, and ethane as the trio for assigning sp, sp², sp³ — and asking which has the shortest, strongest C-C bond (ethyne).

questions.prompt: Hybridization on carbon in ethene (ethylene), CH₂=CH₂.
questions.explanation: In ethene (H₂C=CH₂), each carbon has 3 electron domains — two C-H σ bonds and one C=C σ bond. So each C is sp² hybridized: one 2s + two 2p mix to form three sp² hybrids in a trigonal-planar arrangement at 120°. The leftover unhybridized 2p orbital on each C overlaps sideways with the other C's leftover 2p, forming the π component of the double bond (1 σ + 1 π). The π bond restricts rotation about the C=C axis — the origin of cis/trans isomerism. AP Chemistry Unit 2 expects students to identify σ/π bond counts in any Lewis structure.

questions.prompt: Bond formed by head-on orbital overlap along the internuclear axis.
questions.explanation: A σ (sigma) bond is the head-on, end-to-end overlap of two atomic or hybrid orbitals along the internuclear axis. Electron density concentrates between the nuclei, giving maximum orbital overlap and the strongest of all covalent bonds. Every single bond is a σ; every double bond has one σ + one π; every triple bond has one σ + two π. σ bonds allow free rotation about the bond axis (unlike π bonds). When the AP Chemistry Unit 2 question is 'count σ and π bonds in this Lewis structure,' the correct count of σ bonds equals the count of bonded-atom pairs — including those that also share π bonds.

questions.prompt: Distance between two nuclei at the minimum-energy point of the bond.
questions.explanation: Bond length is the equilibrium distance between two bonded nuclei, where attractive (electron-nucleus) and repulsive (electron-electron, nucleus-nucleus) forces balance to give a minimum-energy configuration on the Morse-like potential well. Shorter bonds are stronger: C-C 154 pm (347 kJ/mol), C=C 134 pm (614 kJ/mol), C≡C 120 pm (839 kJ/mol). Bond length increases down a group (larger atomic radii) and decreases with increasing bond order. AP Chemistry Unit 2 rubrics expect students to invoke bond order and atomic radii when ranking bond lengths or strengths.

questions.prompt: Energy required to break one mole of bonds in the gas phase; always endothermic.
questions.explanation: Bond enthalpy (or bond dissociation energy) is the energy required to homolytically break 1 mole of a particular bond in the gas phase. Breaking bonds is always endothermic (positive ΔH); forming bonds is always exothermic. AP Chemistry uses average bond enthalpies in approximation calculations: ΔH_rxn ≈ Σ(bonds broken) − Σ(bonds formed). Triple bonds (N≡N 945, C≡C 839, C≡O 1080 kJ/mol) sit at the top. AP Chemistry Unit 2 free-response routinely tests this ΔH calculation, especially comparing combustion energies of small organic molecules.

questions.prompt: Number of bonding electron pairs between two atoms; double bond has this value of 2.
questions.explanation: Bond order = number of shared electron pairs between two atoms = ½(bonding electrons − antibonding electrons) in MO theory. Single bond = 1, double = 2, triple = 3. Resonance averages bond order over equivalent structures: NO₃⁻ has three resonance forms, so each N-O bond has order (2+1+1)/3 = 1.33. Higher bond order = shorter, stronger bond. AP Chemistry Unit 2 free-response often presents a molecule with delocalized π electrons (NO₃⁻, CO₃²⁻, O₃, benzene) and asks students to compute and use the fractional bond order to predict bond length and reactivity.

questions.prompt: Trend: as bond order increases from 1 to 3, bond length does this.
questions.explanation: As bond order rises (single → double → triple), more shared electron pairs are concentrated between the nuclei, pulling them closer and shortening the bond. For C-C bonds: single 154 pm, double 134 pm, triple 120 pm. Simultaneously, bond enthalpy increases roughly proportionally: 347 → 614 → 839 kJ/mol. Bond length and bond strength have an inverse correlation across these orders for the same pair of atoms — a near-universal trend. AP Chemistry Unit 2 free-response asks students to apply this trend when comparing related species: C=O vs C-O (carbonyl vs alcohol), N=N vs N≡N, etc.

questions.prompt: Triple-bond example: this molecule has 945 kJ/mol bond energy and is inert at STP.
questions.explanation: N₂ has a triple bond (N≡N, bond order 3, length 110 pm) with bond enthalpy ≈ 945 kJ/mol — one of the strongest bonds in nature. Each N atom contributes 5 valence electrons; the Lewis structure shows three shared pairs plus a lone pair on each N. This enormous bond energy makes N₂ kinetically and thermodynamically inert at STP — biological systems rely on nitrogenase enzymes or industrial Haber-Bosch (high P, T, Fe catalyst) to break it. AP Chemistry Unit 2 (and Unit 6) uses N₂ as the prototypical strong-bond, low-reactivity case study.

questions.prompt: IMF between molecules with permanent dipoles, like HCl-HCl.
questions.explanation: Dipole-dipole forces arise between polar molecules: the δ⁺ end of one molecule attracts the δ⁻ end of another. Strength scales with dipole moment magnitude. Acetone (μ = 2.88 D, BP 56°C) has stronger dipole-dipole forces than diethyl ether (μ = 1.15 D, BP 34.6°C). Both have similar molar masses, so dipole-dipole dominates over LDF differences. AP Chemistry Unit 3 rubrics expect students to: (1) identify polar molecules, (2) use bond and molecular dipoles, and (3) rank IMFs accurately. Many free-response questions present two similar-mass molecules to isolate dipole-dipole as the deciding factor.

questions.prompt: Phrase 'like dissolves like' summarizes this principle.
questions.explanation: 'Like dissolves like' captures the empirical solubility rule: polar solvents (water, methanol) dissolve polar/ionic solutes; nonpolar solvents (hexane, CCl₄) dissolve nonpolar solutes (oils, fats, iodine). Dissolution occurs when solute-solvent IMFs are comparable to or stronger than solute-solute and solvent-solvent IMFs — only then can the entropy gain on mixing offset any IMF-disruption enthalpy cost. AP Chemistry Unit 3 free-response routinely demands the four-IMF comparison (solute-solute, solvent-solvent, solute-solvent, plus geometry/polarity) when predicting solubility — never reduce to a one-word rule.

questions.prompt: Curved liquid surface in a tube, concave for water in glass.
questions.explanation: A meniscus is the curved surface of a liquid in a tube, caused by competition between cohesive forces (liquid-liquid IMFs) and adhesive forces (liquid-container IMFs). Water in glass shows a concave meniscus because adhesion (H-bonds with SiO₂) exceeds cohesion. Mercury in glass shows a convex meniscus because metallic cohesion exceeds adhesion to glass. Capillary action — water rising in a thin tube — uses the same principle. AP Chemistry lab protocols (and Unit 3 prompts) require reading the bottom of a concave meniscus for water and the top of a convex meniscus for mercury at eye level.

questions.prompt: IMF between a permanent dipole and an induced dipole in a polarizable neighbor.
questions.explanation: Dipole-induced dipole (Debye) forces occur when a polar molecule distorts the electron cloud of a nonpolar neighbor, inducing a temporary dipole. Strength scales with the polar molecule's dipole moment and the nonpolar molecule's polarizability. This is why O₂ is more soluble in liquid water than N₂ — water's permanent dipole induces a slightly larger dipole in O₂ (more polarizable than N₂). It is also why nonpolar gases dissolve more readily in polar solvents than ideal-gas behavior predicts. AP Chemistry Unit 3 includes dipole-induced-dipole as one of the four major IMFs in the official rubric language.

questions.prompt: Two corrections in the van der Waals equation account for these two ideal-gas failures.
questions.explanation: Johannes van der Waals (Nobel 1910) modified the ideal-gas law to (P + an²/V²)(V − nb) = nRT. The 'a' correction adds back the pressure lost to attractive IMFs (which pull gas molecules inward, reducing wall impacts). The 'b' correction subtracts the volume actually occupied by the gas molecules themselves (which is not free for motion). Real gases deviate from ideal behavior most at high P (molecules close-packed, 'b' matters) and low T (IMFs dominate, 'a' matters). AP Chemistry Unit 3 tests recognition of both correction terms and when ideal-gas approximations fail.

questions.prompt: Weakest intermolecular force, present in every molecule, arising from temporary dipoles.
questions.explanation: London dispersion forces (LDF), described by Fritz London in 1930, are the universal IMF: temporary fluctuations in electron distribution create instantaneous dipoles that induce dipoles in neighbors. Strength scales with polarizability — bigger, more electron-rich species have stronger LDF. That is why noble-gas boiling points rise He < Ar < Kr < Xe, and why I₂ is a solid while Cl₂ is a gas at room T. Branching reduces surface contact and weakens LDF — n-pentane (BP 36°C) > neopentane (BP 9.5°C). AP Chemistry Unit 3 expects polarizability + surface-area reasoning for all LDF comparisons.

questions.prompt: Equation of state PV = nRT linking pressure, volume, moles, and temperature.
questions.explanation: The ideal gas law PV = nRT was assembled by combining Boyle's (1662), Charles's (1787), and Avogadro's (1811) gas laws — Émile Clapeyron stated it explicitly in 1834. R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) = 0.0821 L·atm/(mol·K). It assumes (1) point-particle gas molecules (zero volume), (2) no IMFs, and (3) elastic collisions. AP Chemistry Unit 3 free-response routinely uses PV = nRT for stoichiometric gas-yield problems, molar mass from gas density (M = dRT/P), and partial-pressure calculations. Knowing which R-value to use given units is a frequent rubric point.

questions.prompt: Special dipole-dipole IMF where H bonded to F, O, or N attracts a lone pair on a neighbor.
questions.explanation: Hydrogen bonds are a strong subset of dipole-dipole interactions, formed when H covalently bonded to F, O, or N (the H-bond donor) interacts with a lone pair on F, O, or N of another molecule (the acceptor). Strength ~5-30 kJ/mol — much weaker than covalent bonds but the strongest IMF. Hydrogen bonds explain water's anomalously high BP (100°C vs −60°C for H₂S), the structure of DNA's double helix, protein secondary structure (α-helix, β-sheet), and ice being less dense than liquid water. AP Chemistry Unit 3 free-response often hinges on identifying H-bonding correctly.

questions.prompt: Direct conversion from solid to gas without going through the liquid phase.
questions.explanation: Sublimation is the direct phase change from solid to gas at constant temperature, bypassing the liquid phase, when the solid's vapor pressure exceeds atmospheric pressure below its triple-point temperature. Dry ice (CO₂(s), sublimes at −78.5°C, 1 atm), iodine, naphthalene (mothballs), and water ice (slowly, in freezer-burn) all sublime. ΔH_sublimation = ΔH_fusion + ΔH_vaporization (Hess's law). AP Chemistry Unit 3 free-response uses sublimation for phase-diagram interpretation and Hess's-law calculations relating the three phase-transition enthalpies.

questions.prompt: IMF responsible for ice being less dense than liquid water near 0°C.
questions.explanation: In ice, each water molecule hydrogen-bonds to four neighbors in a tetrahedral lattice — an open hexagonal arrangement that holds molecules farther apart than in liquid water. When ice melts, ~10% of H-bonds break, the lattice collapses, and water molecules pack more closely — so liquid water (1.000 g/mL at 4°C) is denser than ice (0.917 g/mL at 0°C). This anomalous density is why ice floats, why lakes freeze top-down (preserving aquatic life), and why bursting pipes is a winter problem. AP Chemistry Unit 3 uses the ice density anomaly as the canonical hydrogen-bond consequence.

questions.prompt: State with fixed shape and volume, particles vibrate in place.
questions.explanation: Solids hold both shape and volume because intermolecular forces dominate over thermal kinetic energy — particles vibrate around fixed lattice (crystalline) or random (amorphous) positions. Crystalline solids exhibit sharp melting points (NaCl 801°C), long-range order (X-ray diffraction patterns), and anisotropic properties. Amorphous solids (glass, plastics) soften gradually because they lack long-range order. AP Chemistry Unit 3 distinguishes molecular, ionic, covalent-network, and metallic solids — each with characteristic properties traceable to the type of bonding holding the lattice together (a core 'classify-the-solid' free-response prompt).

questions.prompt: Inverse relationship: at constant T, P increases as V decreases.
questions.explanation: Boyle's law (Robert Boyle, 1662) states that at constant temperature and moles, P × V = constant, so P₁V₁ = P₂V₂. Squeezing a gas to half its volume doubles its pressure. The law applies to ideal gases and to real gases at low pressures and high temperatures (far from condensation). At high P or low T, deviations grow because intermolecular attractions and molecular volume become significant. AP Chemistry Unit 3 routinely tests Boyle's law in scuba-diving, syringe-compression, or balloon-altitude contexts, then asks students to identify when ideal-gas behavior breaks down.

questions.prompt: Direct relationship: at constant P, V increases linearly with absolute temperature.
questions.explanation: Charles's law (Jacques Charles, 1787; published by Gay-Lussac, 1802) states that at constant pressure and moles, V/T = constant, so V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂. Doubling absolute temperature doubles volume. Linear extrapolation of V vs T to V = 0 gives −273.15°C — historically the first quantitative evidence for absolute zero. AP Chemistry Unit 3 demands the use of Kelvin (never Celsius) for any gas-law calculation, and tests Charles's law in hot-air-balloon and tire-pressure-on-cold-day scenarios. Combining Boyle's + Charles's + Avogadro's gives PV = nRT.

questions.prompt: Law stating gas solubility in liquid is proportional to its partial pressure.
questions.explanation: Henry's law (William Henry, 1803) states C = k_H × P, where C is gas solubility in solution and P is the gas's partial pressure above the solution. k_H depends on the gas and solvent. Examples: opening a soda releases CO₂ because P_CO₂ above the liquid drops sharply; SCUBA divers ascending too quickly risk decompression sickness as dissolved N₂ comes out of solution. Gas solubility decreases as T rises (gas dissolution is exothermic; Le Chatelier favors release). AP Chemistry Unit 3 routinely uses Henry's law in environmental contexts (CO₂ in oceans, O₂ in lakes) and ties to Unit 7 equilibrium reasoning.

questions.prompt: Law stating that total pressure equals the sum of partial pressures of each gas.
questions.explanation: Dalton's law of partial pressures (John Dalton, 1801) states that in a mixture of non-reacting gases, each gas exerts a partial pressure independent of the others, and P_total = ΣP_i. Partial pressure is also P_i = x_i × P_total, where x_i is the mole fraction. In gas-collection-over-water experiments, the collected gas pressure equals atmospheric pressure minus water vapor pressure at the experiment's temperature. AP Chemistry Unit 3 free-response routinely uses Dalton's law in combustion stoichiometry, gas-collection setups, and partial-pressure-driven equilibrium calculations (Unit 7 Kp ties).

questions.prompt: Moles of solute per liter of solution.
questions.explanation: Molarity (M) is moles of solute divided by liters of solution: M = n / V. It is the most-used concentration unit in chemistry, but it depends on temperature because solution volume expands when heated. Preparing 250.0 mL of 0.100 M NaCl requires 0.0250 mol NaCl (1.46 g) dissolved and diluted to the mark in a 250-mL volumetric flask. AP Chemistry Unit 3 free-response routinely tests serial dilution (M₁V₁ = M₂V₂), molarity-from-mass calculations, and stoichiometry in solution — molarity is the bridge between formula-mass problems and titration math (Unit 4).

questions.prompt: State with fixed volume but takes the shape of its container.
questions.explanation: Liquids retain a fixed volume because IMFs still bind molecules together, but particles have enough kinetic energy to flow past each other — so the liquid takes the container's shape. Surface tension (water 72 mN/m at 25°C), viscosity, and capillary action all reflect the strength of IMFs. Liquids are roughly 1000× denser than gases (same molar mass) because molecules are in van der Waals contact. AP Chemistry Unit 3 routinely contrasts liquid vs. gas properties using IMF reasoning, and ties liquid-state behavior to Unit 8 solution chemistry and Unit 6 vaporization enthalpy.

questions.prompt: Separation that purifies a dissolved solid by slow cooling of a saturated solution.
questions.explanation: Recrystallization purifies a solid by dissolving it in a minimum amount of hot solvent, then cooling slowly so the major solid crystallizes out while soluble impurities stay in solution (and insoluble impurities are removed by hot filtration). Solvent choice is critical: the solute must be much more soluble hot than cold; impurities must be either very soluble cold or insoluble at any temperature. Common in organic-chemistry yield workups. AP Chemistry Unit 3 free-response often tests recrystallization in the context of acid-base salt purification or analytical sample preparation.

questions.prompt: Solution property where freezing point drops in proportion to solute mole fraction.
questions.explanation: Freezing-point depression is a colligative property: ΔTf = i × Kf × m, where i is the van't Hoff factor (~1 for nonelectrolytes, ~2 for NaCl, ~3 for CaCl₂), Kf is the solvent's cryoscopic constant (water: 1.86 °C·kg/mol), and m is molality. The solute disrupts the solid lattice, lowering the equilibrium freezing temperature. This is why road salt melts ice (CaCl₂ has i = 3, better than NaCl's i = 2 at the same molality) and antifreeze (ethylene glycol) protects radiators. AP Chemistry Unit 3 tests colligative-property setup and van't-Hoff-factor identification.

questions.prompt: Point on a phase diagram where all three phases coexist in equilibrium.
questions.explanation: The triple point is the unique (T, P) where solid, liquid, and gas phases coexist in equilibrium. Water's triple point is 273.16 K (0.01°C) at 611.657 Pa — used to define the kelvin until 2019. CO₂'s triple point is 216.55 K, 5.18 atm, explaining why dry ice sublimes at 1 atm (we are below its triple-point pressure). Above the critical point (T_c, P_c), gas and liquid become indistinguishable (supercritical fluid). AP Chemistry Unit 3 routinely shows a P-T phase diagram and asks students to identify the triple point, critical point, and conditions of phase transitions.

questions.prompt: Separation technique using a porous barrier to retain solids while liquids pass.
questions.explanation: Filtration separates a heterogeneous solid-liquid mixture by passing it through a porous medium (filter paper, sintered glass). The solid (filtrate residue) stays behind; the liquid (filtrate) passes through. Quantitative filtration uses ashless filter paper for gravimetric analysis. Vacuum filtration (Büchner funnel + side-arm flask) is faster but unsuitable for fine precipitates that might pass through pores. AP Chemistry Unit 3 lists filtration as one of four core separations; rubric for the gravimetric analysis lab demands students explain how filtration isolates a precipitate so it can be massed.

questions.prompt: Separation that splits components by their differing affinity for a stationary phase.
questions.explanation: Chromatography separates components by their differing distributions between a moving (mobile) and stationary phase. Mikhail Tsvet introduced the technique in 1900 to separate plant pigments. In paper chromatography, polar paper acts as the stationary phase, a less-polar solvent climbs by capillary action, and components with different polarities migrate at different rates (Rf = distance traveled by component / distance traveled by solvent). AP Chemistry Unit 3 expects students to interpret Rf values and link migration order to IMF strengths — most polar component sticks closest to polar stationary phase.

questions.prompt: Separation that boils a solution and recovers solvent by condensing its vapor.
questions.explanation: Distillation separates components based on volatility differences. Simple distillation works for mixtures where one component is much more volatile (e.g., water + dissolved solid → pure water as distillate). Fractional distillation is needed when boiling points differ by < ~25°C. Steam distillation uses water vapor to lower the effective boiling point of heat-sensitive organics. AP Chemistry Unit 3 free-response includes distillation among the four named separations and may ask students to choose the appropriate technique given a mixture's components and properties — vapor-pressure logic is the rubric's key.

questions.prompt: Process where two miscible liquids separate by boiling-point difference in a column.
questions.explanation: Fractional distillation separates miscible liquids by exploiting differences in boiling point. Vapor enters a packed (or plate) column where it repeatedly condenses and re-vaporizes — each cycle enriches the vapor in the more volatile component. Used industrially in petroleum refining (separating gasoline, kerosene, diesel from crude oil based on their boiling-point ranges) and in producing 95% ethanol from fermented mash. AP Chemistry Unit 3 added 'separations' to the redesigned CED — fractional distillation, paper chromatography, filtration, and recrystallization are the four named techniques students must explain on free-response.

questions.prompt: Quantity equal to distance traveled by spot divided by solvent-front distance.
questions.explanation: The retention factor Rf in chromatography is Rf = (distance from origin to spot) / (distance from origin to solvent front). Rf values range 0 to 1: components more attracted to the stationary phase have low Rf (stay near origin); more soluble in the mobile phase have high Rf (travel close to the solvent front). Rf depends on stationary-phase polarity, mobile-phase polarity, and analyte structure. AP Chemistry Unit 3 free-response routinely shows a TLC plate and asks students to (1) compute Rf, (2) identify the most/least polar component, and (3) suggest a more-polar solvent to improve separation.

questions.prompt: Reaction that transfers a proton from one species to another.
questions.explanation: Brønsted-Lowry acid-base reactions transfer a proton (H⁺) from an acid (donor) to a base (acceptor). Example: HCl(aq) + NH₃(aq) → NH₄⁺(aq) + Cl⁻(aq). Conjugate acid-base pairs differ by exactly one H⁺. Strong acid + strong base gives a neutralization to a salt and water (HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O, ΔH ≈ −55.8 kJ/mol for any strong-acid strong-base combo). AP Chemistry Unit 4 introduces these and they recur in Unit 8 (pH, buffers, titration curves) and Unit 7 (Ka, Kb equilibria). Net-ionic equations are required for nearly every acid-base free response.

questions.prompt: Reactant fully consumed first, capping the maximum product yield.
questions.explanation: The limiting reactant (LR) is the species fully consumed first in a reaction, so it caps the maximum possible product yield. To identify: compute moles of each reactant, divide by their stoichiometric coefficients, and the smallest ratio is the LR. Example: 4 g H₂ + 32 g O₂ →? H₂O. H₂: 4/2 ÷ 2 = 1; O₂: 32/32 ÷ 1 = 1 — they are stoichiometric (no excess). AP Chemistry Unit 4 free-response routinely tests LR identification before any percent-yield calculation. Mole-ratio-divided-by-coefficient is the rubric-preferred method, not 'gram-comparison.'.

questions.prompt: Ratio of actual yield to theoretical yield, expressed as a percent.
questions.explanation: Percent yield = (actual yield / theoretical yield) × 100%. Theoretical yield is calculated from the limiting reactant assuming complete reaction; actual yield is measured. Yields below 100% are common (side reactions, transfer losses, incomplete conversion, equilibrium). Yields above 100% indicate an experimental error — wet product, side-reaction contamination, or wrong identification. AP Chemistry Unit 4 free-response routinely scores three steps: (1) identify the limiting reactant, (2) compute theoretical yield, (3) compute percent yield. Students who skip step 1 typically lose 2 of 3 points.

questions.prompt: Reaction class for hydrocarbon + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O, releasing heat.
questions.explanation: Combustion is the rapid reaction of a fuel (typically a hydrocarbon or other organic) with oxygen, releasing heat and light. Complete combustion of a CxHy gives x CO₂ + (y/2) H₂O. Methane: CH₄ + 2 O₂ → CO₂ + 2 H₂O, ΔH = −890.4 kJ/mol. Incomplete combustion (insufficient O₂) produces CO (carbon monoxide) or even C (soot) — significant for indoor air-quality and engine-efficiency questions. AP Chemistry Unit 4 uses combustion for balancing practice and Unit 6 for ΔH_combustion calorimetry calculations; bomb calorimeters measure exactly these enthalpies.

questions.prompt: Species that gives up electrons in a redox reaction.
questions.explanation: A reducing agent (reductant) loses electrons (is oxidized itself) and donates them to whatever it is reducing. Strong reducing agents: alkali metals (Li, Na, K), active metals (Al, Zn, Fe), H₂ gas, hydrides (LiAlH₄, NaBH₄). The activity series (Li > K > Ca > Na > Mg > Al > Zn > Fe > Cu > Ag) lists metals from strongest reducer (top) to weakest. In Zn + Cu²⁺ → Zn²⁺ + Cu, Zn is the reducing agent. AP Chemistry Unit 4 (and Unit 9, electrochemistry) routinely asks 'which is the stronger reducing agent and why?' — students must cite standard reduction potentials.

questions.prompt: 6.022 × 10²³, the number of particles in one mole.
questions.explanation: Avogadro's number, NA = 6.02214076 × 10²³ mol⁻¹, is the number of elementary entities (atoms, molecules, ions) in one mole. It was named for Amedeo Avogadro (1811 hypothesis: equal volumes of gases at the same T, P contain equal numbers of molecules) but determined by Jean Perrin and Robert Millikan a century later. Since the 2019 SI redefinition, NA is now a defined exact number. AP Chemistry Unit 4 uses NA to convert between moles and individual particles, and it underpins every mole-scale calculation in stoichiometry, gas laws, and electrochemistry (Faraday's constant F = NA × e).

questions.prompt: Substance dissolved in a larger amount of solvent.
questions.explanation: In a solution, the solute is the substance present in smaller amount (or the one whose phase changes upon mixing), and the solvent is the major component (usually liquid). For aqueous solutions, water is the solvent; NaCl, ethanol, glucose are common solutes. Concentration units relate amount of solute to amount of solution or solvent: molarity (mol solute / L solution), molality (mol solute / kg solvent), mass percent, and mole fraction. AP Chemistry Unit 4 and Unit 3 both require fluency converting between these units, especially when colligative properties (Unit 3) or molarity-based stoichiometry (Unit 4) are involved.

questions.prompt: Empirical formula of a compound with 40.0% C, 6.7% H, 53.3% O by mass.
questions.explanation: Assume 100 g sample: 40.0 g C, 6.7 g H, 53.3 g O. Moles: C = 40.0/12.01 = 3.33; H = 6.7/1.008 = 6.65; O = 53.3/16.00 = 3.33. Divide by smallest (3.33): C = 1, H = 2, O = 1. Empirical formula = CH₂O (formaldehyde or any carbohydrate building block, like glucose's C₆H₁₂O₆). Molecular formula = (CH₂O)_n where n = molar mass / 30.03. AP Chemistry Unit 4 routinely asks for empirical and molecular formulas from mass-percent data — sometimes combined with combustion-analysis data for the C and H content.

questions.prompt: Point in a titration where moles of acid and base exactly match.
questions.explanation: The equivalence point is where moles of titrant exactly match the stoichiometric ratio for the moles of analyte. For strong-acid strong-base titration, the equivalence point pH = 7 because only a neutral salt and water remain. Weak-acid strong-base titrations have equivalence point pH > 7 (conjugate base hydrolyzes). Weak-base strong-acid titrations have pH < 7. The endpoint (indicator color change) approximates but is not identical to the true equivalence point — indicator choice is critical. AP Chemistry Unit 4 (and Unit 8) requires students to compute equivalence-point pH and identify it on a titration curve.

questions.prompt: Equation balanced for atoms and charge on both sides.
questions.explanation: A balanced chemical equation conserves both mass (number of each element's atoms) and charge (total ionic charge) on both sides. To balance: count atoms of each element, adjust coefficients (never subscripts), check polyatomic ions as units when possible, balance H and O last for combustion. For redox, balance electrons separately (half-reaction method, balance in acidic/basic solution as needed). Example: 3 Cu + 8 HNO₃ → 3 Cu(NO₃)₂ + 2 NO + 4 H₂O. AP Chemistry Unit 4 free-response always requires balanced equations — unbalanced equations earn zero on stoichiometry questions.

questions.prompt: Reaction forming an insoluble solid from two aqueous solutions.
questions.explanation: A precipitation reaction occurs when two aqueous solutions combine to form an insoluble ionic solid (precipitate). Example: AgNO₃(aq) + NaCl(aq) → AgCl(s) + NaNO₃(aq). Predicting precipitates requires the solubility rules (Group 1 and ammonium salts always soluble; most nitrates soluble; most halides soluble except Ag⁺, Pb²⁺, Hg₂²⁺; most sulfates soluble except Sr²⁺, Ba²⁺, Pb²⁺; most carbonates, phosphates, sulfides insoluble except Group 1 and ammonium). AP Chemistry Unit 4 free-response routinely demands net-ionic equations for these — spectator ions removed.

questions.prompt: Solution that conducts electricity due to dissolved ions.
questions.explanation: An electrolyte is a substance that dissociates in solution to give ions that conduct electricity. Strong electrolytes (NaCl, HCl, NaOH) fully dissociate; weak electrolytes (acetic acid, NH₃) only partially dissociate; nonelectrolytes (sugar, ethanol) don't dissociate. Conductivity scales with ion concentration and mobility. Pure water is a poor conductor (only 10⁻⁷ M H⁺ and OH⁻ at 25°C). AP Chemistry Unit 4 tests electrolyte vs. nonelectrolyte recognition for solubility-rule problems, and it underpins acid-base behavior in Unit 8 and electrochemistry in Unit 9.

questions.prompt: Procedure of adding a measured volume of one solution to react completely with another.
questions.explanation: A titration delivers a precise volume of a known-concentration solution (titrant) from a buret into a measured volume of unknown-concentration solution (analyte), until equivalence — the moles ratio matches the balanced equation. Endpoint (indicator color change) approximates equivalence point (true stoichiometric mole match). Acid-base titrations use phenolphthalein (colorless → pink at pH ~8-10) or methyl orange (red → yellow at pH 3-4). Redox titrations use KMnO₄ (purple → colorless) or starch-iodine. AP Chemistry Unit 4 free-response demands setup, calculation, and a labeled titration curve (Unit 8 polishes this further).

questions.prompt: Equation M₁V₁ = M₂V₂ used to compute concentration after this process.
questions.explanation: Dilution adds solvent to a solution to reduce solute concentration without changing total moles of solute. Since n_solute is conserved: M₁V₁ = M₂V₂. Example: diluting 25.0 mL of 6.0 M HCl to 250.0 mL gives M₂ = (6.0 × 25.0)/250.0 = 0.60 M. Serial dilution multiplies dilution factors (10× dilutions in 4 stages → 10,000× total). AP Chemistry Unit 4 free-response routinely tests dilution math, sometimes embedded inside a multi-step titration or kinetics setup. Wrong-volume traps include using initial vs. added vs. final volume — the formula always uses final total volume.

questions.prompt: Spectator-ion-free chemical equation Pb²⁺(aq) + 2 I⁻(aq) → PbI₂(s) — for this reaction.
questions.explanation: Pb²⁺(aq) + 2 I⁻(aq) → PbI₂(s) is the famous 'golden rain' demonstration: mixing colorless Pb(NO₃)₂ and KI solutions yields a brilliant yellow PbI₂ precipitate (Ksp = 7.1 × 10⁻⁹). Hot water dissolves PbI₂; cooling re-crystallizes it as glittering hexagonal plates. AP Chemistry Unit 4 free-response uses this reaction for net-ionic equation practice and Unit 7 for Ksp calculations. The full molecular equation Pb(NO₃)₂ + 2 KI → PbI₂ + 2 KNO₃ shows K⁺ and NO₃⁻ as spectators. Mass-percent and limiting-reactant analyses of this system are 2-3 point free-response staples.

questions.prompt: Charge an atom would have if all bonds were ionic.
questions.explanation: Oxidation number (or state) assigns a hypothetical charge to each atom assuming all bonds are 100% ionic. Rules: free elements 0; monoatomic ions = ion charge; F always −1 in compounds; O usually −2 (peroxides −1, OF₂ +2); H usually +1 (metal hydrides −1); sum equals overall charge of species. Example: in SO₄²⁻, O is −2 (×4 = −8), so S must be +6. AP Chemistry Unit 4 free-response demands oxidation-number assignment before any redox analysis — wrong ON usually torpedoes the whole problem.

questions.prompt: Process where an atom loses electrons.
questions.explanation: Oxidation is the loss of electrons from a species (mnemonic: 'OIL' — oxidation is loss). The oxidized species' oxidation number increases. Antoine Lavoisier originally coined 'oxidation' for reaction with oxygen, but the modern definition is general — any electron loss qualifies. In Zn + Cu²⁺ → Zn²⁺ + Cu, Zn is oxidized (0 → +2, losing 2 e⁻) and acts as the reducing agent. AP Chemistry Unit 4 expects students to write the oxidation half-reaction (Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2 e⁻) and identify both the species oxidized and the reducing agent — these are different rubric points.

questions.prompt: Reaction that transfers electrons; one species is oxidized, the other reduced.
questions.explanation: Redox (reduction-oxidation) reactions involve electron transfer. The species losing electrons is oxidized (its oxidation number rises) and acts as the reducing agent; the species gaining electrons is reduced (oxidation number falls) and acts as the oxidizing agent. Example: 2 Na + Cl₂ → 2 NaCl — Na is oxidized (0 → +1, RA); Cl is reduced (0 → −1, OA). AP Chemistry Unit 4 expects students to assign oxidation numbers, identify what is oxidized vs reduced, name the agents, and balance half-reactions in acidic or basic solution — a near-annual free-response staple.

questions.prompt: Process where an atom gains electrons; opposite of oxidation.
questions.explanation: Reduction is the gain of electrons (mnemonic: 'RIG' — reduction is gain; combined with OIL gives OILRIG). The reduced species' oxidation number decreases (becomes more negative or less positive). In Zn + Cu²⁺ → Zn²⁺ + Cu, Cu²⁺ is reduced (+2 → 0, gaining 2 e⁻) and acts as the oxidizing agent. Common oxidizing agents: O₂, MnO₄⁻ (purple), Cr₂O₇²⁻ (orange), H₂O₂. Common reducing agents: H₂, active metals, Sn²⁺. AP Chemistry Unit 4 tests recognition of both species: 'identify the species reduced AND the oxidizing agent' is two different rubric points.

questions.prompt: Special redox reaction where a single element is both oxidized and reduced.
questions.explanation: Disproportionation is a redox reaction where a single element in a single oxidation state is both oxidized and reduced. Classic example: 2 H₂O₂ → 2 H₂O + O₂. The oxygen in H₂O₂ is −1; in H₂O it is −2 (reduced) and in O₂ it is 0 (oxidized). Another: 3 Cl₂ + 6 NaOH → 5 NaCl + NaClO₃ + 3 H₂O (Cl goes from 0 to −1 and to +5). Disproportionation requires that the intermediate oxidation state is thermodynamically unstable relative to higher and lower states. AP Chemistry Unit 4 free-response routinely tests recognition and balancing of disproportionations.

questions.prompt: Lab technique that determines composition by precipitation, drying, and massing.
questions.explanation: Gravimetric analysis determines the amount of a substance by precipitating it as a pure compound, isolating the precipitate by filtration, drying it (to constant mass to confirm completeness), and massing. The mass of precipitate is converted to the original analyte mass via the formula's mole ratio. Classic example: determining sulfate by precipitating BaSO₄ (Ksp ≈ 10⁻¹⁰), then weighing. AP Chemistry Unit 4 lists gravimetric analysis as a key lab skill and free-response routinely walks students through (1) precipitation, (2) filtration, (3) drying-to-constant-mass, (4) calculation steps.

questions.prompt: Step that removes water from a precipitate to find its dry mass.
questions.explanation: Drying a precipitate removes adsorbed water before massing. The 'dry to constant mass' protocol weighs the sample, dries it again (typically 100-110°C oven, ~30 min), reweighs, and repeats until two successive weighings agree within 0.001 g. This ensures all water is gone without thermal decomposition of the precipitate. Hygroscopic precipitates (those that re-absorb water during cooling) must be cooled in desiccators. AP Chemistry Unit 4 rubrics for gravimetric labs award separate points for (1) explaining why drying is necessary and (2) describing the constant-mass criterion.

questions.prompt: Pre-massing step where a beaker or filter is heated and cooled until its mass stops changing.
questions.explanation: Before adding analyte, the empty container (crucible, beaker, filter paper) is conditioned: heated under conditions identical to those used later, cooled in a desiccator, and weighed. The cycle repeats until the empty container mass is constant. Only then is the analyte added — so any subsequent mass change can be attributed solely to the precipitate, not to water gain/loss by the container. AP Chemistry Unit 4 free-response routinely awards a point for this conditioning step, often paired with a question on why a student got too-high or too-low results (often: failed to tare to constant mass).

questions.prompt: Salt class typically used to precipitate chloride for gravimetric analysis.
questions.explanation: Gravimetric chloride determination uses Ag⁺ (from AgNO₃) to precipitate AgCl (Ksp = 1.8 × 10⁻¹⁰): Cl⁻(aq) + Ag⁺(aq) → AgCl(s). The white precipitate is filtered, washed (with dilute HNO₃ to dissolve any Ag₂O contamination), dried, and massed. Mole ratio 1:1, so n_Cl⁻ = n_AgCl, and grams of Cl⁻ = n_AgCl × 35.45. AP Chemistry Unit 4 free-response has used silver-chloride gravimetric labs as the calibration question for analytical accuracy, asking students to identify and quantify sources of error — for example, incomplete drying inflates measured chloride.

questions.prompt: Equation that shows only species that change, omitting spectator ions.
questions.explanation: A net ionic equation shows only the species that participate in the chemical change — spectator ions (those appearing on both sides unchanged) are removed. To derive: (1) write the balanced molecular equation, (2) dissociate all strong electrolytes (strong acids, strong bases, soluble salts) into ions, (3) cancel ions that appear identically on both sides. For AgNO₃ + NaCl → AgCl + NaNO₃, the NIE is Ag⁺(aq) + Cl⁻(aq) → AgCl(s). AP Chemistry Unit 4 free-response routinely scores 1 point each for the molecular, complete-ionic, and net-ionic forms — accuracy is non-negotiable.

questions.prompt: Equation that expresses reaction rate as a function of reactant concentrations.
questions.explanation: A rate law expresses reaction rate as rate = k[A]^m[B]^n, where k is the rate constant, m and n are the orders in A and B (determined experimentally, not from coefficients), and [A], [B] are concentrations. Overall order = m + n. The rate law cannot be predicted from the balanced equation — only the elementary steps of the mechanism give rate-law exponents matching their molecularity. AP Chemistry Unit 5 free-response routinely supplies initial-rates data, asks students to determine each order, the value of k (with units), and the overall order — a 4-point staple.

questions.prompt: Linear plot for a first-order reaction: ln[A] vs this variable.
questions.explanation: For a first-order reaction, the integrated rate law is ln[A]_t = −kt + ln[A]_0. A plot of ln[A] vs time is linear with slope = −k and y-intercept = ln[A]_0. Zero order: [A] vs t is linear, slope −k. Second order: 1/[A] vs t is linear, slope +k. Identifying the linear plot from experimental data is the textbook method for determining reaction order. AP Chemistry Unit 5 free-response routinely supplies a data table and asks students to test all three plots, identify which is linear, and conclude the order — then read k from the slope.

questions.prompt: Theory that reaction rate depends on collision frequency, geometry, and energy.
questions.explanation: Collision theory states that reactions occur when molecules collide with (1) sufficient kinetic energy to surpass Ea, (2) correct orientation (steric factor), and (3) enough frequency. Rate = (collision frequency) × (energy factor e^(−Ea/RT)) × (steric factor p). Raising T increases the fraction of molecules with E > Ea (Maxwell-Boltzmann tail), exponentially boosting rate. AP Chemistry Unit 5 free-response routinely uses collision theory to explain why doubling concentration doubles rate (more collisions) and why increasing T raises rate more steeply than the simple sqrt-T collision-frequency increase would predict — Ea barrier dominates.

questions.prompt: Method that finds reaction order by comparing trials with one concentration changed.
questions.explanation: The method of initial rates determines reaction orders by running multiple trials in which one reactant concentration is changed while others stay constant. Take the ratio of rates: if doubling [A] doubles rate, order in A is 1 (2¹); if rate quadruples, order is 2 (2²); if rate doesn't change, order is 0 (2⁰). Repeat for each reactant. Then plug any trial's data into rate = k[A]^m[B]^n to solve for k. AP Chemistry Unit 5 free-response routinely supplies 3-5 trials and demands students systematically compute each order — a near-annual 5-point question.

questions.prompt: Distribution showing what fraction of molecules at a given T has each kinetic energy.
questions.explanation: The Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution gives the probability of a molecule having a given speed (or kinetic energy) at temperature T. Curves shift right and broaden as T rises; lighter molecules at the same T have curves peaked at higher speeds. The fraction of molecules with E > Ea is the area under the curve to the right of Ea — this fraction grows exponentially with T, which is why a 10°C rise often doubles reaction rate near room T. AP Chemistry Unit 5 free-response asks students to (1) sketch curves at two temperatures, (2) mark Ea, and (3) explain why the area to the right of Ea increases sharply with T.

questions.prompt: Sequence of elementary steps adding up to the overall reaction.
questions.explanation: A reaction mechanism is the step-by-step molecular pathway by which reactants become products. Each step is an elementary reaction whose rate law follows directly from its molecularity (unimolecular: rate = k[A]; bimolecular: rate = k[A][B]). Steps sum to give the overall balanced equation, with intermediates (formed in one step, consumed in a later one) canceling out. The slowest step determines the overall rate law. AP Chemistry Unit 5 free-response routinely supplies a proposed mechanism and asks students to (1) verify it sums to the overall equation, (2) identify intermediates, and (3) derive the predicted rate law from the rate-determining step.

questions.prompt: Slowest elementary step that controls the overall reaction rate.
questions.explanation: The rate-determining step (RDS) is the slowest elementary step in a multi-step mechanism. Just as a chain is only as strong as its weakest link, a reaction is only as fast as its slowest step. The RDS's rate law (built from its molecularity, not the overall equation) becomes the experimental rate law of the whole reaction. If the RDS is the first step, the rate law uses initial reactants directly; if the RDS is a later step, intermediates must be substituted out using fast-equilibrium expressions from earlier steps. AP Chemistry Unit 5 routinely tests this substitution — a near-annual mechanism free-response.

questions.prompt: Time for the concentration of a first-order reactant to fall to half its initial value.
questions.explanation: Half-life (t_½) is the time for a reactant's concentration to fall to half its initial value. For first-order reactions, t_½ = ln(2)/k = 0.693/k — a constant independent of starting concentration. This is why radioactive decay (always first-order) has a fixed half-life: ¹⁴C is 5730 yr; ²³⁸U is 4.47 × 10⁹ yr. For zero-order, t_½ = [A]_0/(2k); for second-order, t_½ = 1/(k[A]_0) — both concentration-dependent. AP Chemistry Unit 5 free-response routinely tests the first-order half-life formula and radiocarbon-dating-style calculations.

questions.prompt: Sum of all elementary steps must yield this with intermediates canceled.
questions.explanation: A proposed mechanism is valid only if (1) each step is plausible (uni-, bi-, rarely termolecular), (2) the rate law derived from the RDS matches the experimental rate law, and (3) the elementary steps add — with intermediates canceling — to give the overall balanced equation. To check: list each step's species, cancel anything that appears on both sides equally (intermediates), and confirm the remaining reactants and products match the overall stoichiometry. AP Chemistry Unit 5 free-response always tests this step-by-step verification, often paired with asking why a given mechanism is or isn't consistent with the experimental rate law.

questions.prompt: Energy hill that reactants must climb to become products.
questions.explanation: Activation energy (Ea) is the minimum kinetic energy that colliding molecules must have to overcome the energy barrier and form products. On a potential-energy diagram, it is the height from reactants to the transition state. Typical small-molecule organic reactions have Ea ~50-150 kJ/mol; biological reactions ~30-50 kJ/mol thanks to enzyme catalysis. Lower Ea = faster reaction at any given temperature. Ea is unchanged whether the reaction is exothermic or endothermic — Ea only sets the rate; ΔH sets the position of products relative to reactants. AP Chemistry Unit 5 expects students to label Ea on every PE diagram drawn.

questions.prompt: High-energy fleeting species at the top of the Ea barrier.
questions.explanation: The transition state (activated complex) is the highest-energy configuration along the reaction coordinate — bonds are partially broken and partially formed. It is a saddle point on the potential-energy surface (not a minimum), so it has zero lifetime; unlike intermediates, transition states can't be isolated. Represented in mechanism diagrams as [reactants‡] with partial bonds. Eyring's transition-state theory (1935) provides a more rigorous alternative to Arrhenius for predicting rate constants. AP Chemistry Unit 5 free-response asks students to mark the transition state and intermediates (if any) on a PE diagram, distinguishing them carefully.

questions.prompt: Slope of an Arrhenius plot (ln k vs 1/T) divided by R gives this quantity.
questions.explanation: Taking ln of Arrhenius gives ln(k) = ln(A) − Ea/(RT) = ln(A) − (Ea/R)(1/T). Plotting ln(k) vs 1/T gives a straight line with slope = −Ea/R and y-intercept = ln(A). So Ea = −R × slope. For two data points: ln(k₂/k₁) = −(Ea/R)(1/T₂ − 1/T₁) — the most-asked Arrhenius algebra problem on AP Chemistry Unit 5 free-response. Students plug in k₁ at T₁, k₂ at T₂, and solve for Ea. Common pitfall: forgetting that R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) requires Ea in J/mol, not kJ/mol.

questions.prompt: Plot of reaction progress vs energy, showing reactants, products, and Ea.
questions.explanation: A potential-energy (PE) diagram plots energy on the y-axis against reaction coordinate on the x-axis. Key features: reactants (left), transition state(s) (peaks), intermediates (valleys), products (right). ΔH = E_products − E_reactants (exothermic if negative, endothermic if positive). Ea_forward = E_TS − E_reactants; Ea_reverse = E_TS − E_products. Multi-step reactions show multiple humps. Catalysts lower all activation barriers; they do NOT change ΔH. AP Chemistry Unit 5 free-response routinely asks students to draw, label, and interpret PE diagrams — often comparing catalyzed vs uncatalyzed cases.

questions.prompt: Species that speeds a reaction by lowering activation energy without being consumed.
questions.explanation: A catalyst speeds up a reaction by providing an alternative mechanism with lower activation energy. It is consumed in one step and regenerated in a later one, so net stoichiometry is unchanged. Catalysts do NOT change ΔG, ΔH, ΔS, K_eq, or product yield — only kinetics. Homogeneous catalysts (same phase as reactants, e.g., H⁺(aq) in ester hydrolysis) and heterogeneous (different phase, e.g., Pt in catalytic converters) both work by stabilizing transition states or providing easier intermediates. AP Chemistry Unit 5 free-response routinely shows two PE diagrams (catalyzed vs uncatalyzed) and asks students to identify which is lower.

questions.prompt: Type of catalyst that is in the same phase as the reactants.
questions.explanation: Homogeneous catalysts share the same phase as the reactants (typically both dissolved in a solvent). Examples: H⁺(aq) catalyzing ester hydrolysis, MnO₂(aq) catalyzing H₂O₂ decomposition, transition-metal complexes (Wilkinson's catalyst for hydrogenation). They form intermediates that participate in the rate-determining step but are regenerated by the end. Advantages: easy mixing and uniform reactivity. Disadvantages: hard to separate catalyst from products. AP Chemistry Unit 5 free-response routinely compares the energetics and PE diagrams of homogeneous vs heterogeneous catalysis.

questions.prompt: Type of catalyst in a different phase from reactants, like solid Pt with gas H₂.
questions.explanation: Heterogeneous catalysts exist in a different phase from reactants — typically solid catalysts with gas or liquid reactants.
